title: Swiss roll with jam and cream
subtitle: The rolled sponge that has to be rolled while it is still warm
excerpt: A 33 × 23 cm Swiss roll: a light, eggy sponge baked in 12 minutes, turned out while still warm onto sugared paper, rolled up without filling to set the shape, then cooled, unrolled, spread with jam and whipped cream, and rolled again. Serves 8.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the Swiss roll chapter documents the rolled sponge technique, noting the critical warm-roll method that sets the shape without cracking. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: Acton's sponge cake section documents the whisked sponge method used here: eggs and sugar beaten to a ribbon before the flour is folded in. The jam-and-cream filling is the standard British Swiss roll convention documented across Victorian domestic cookery literature.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The rolled unfilled sponge can be refrigerated overnight; unroll carefully and fill on the day of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, top third of oven — speed is the point, not colour

recipeTools.notes: 33 × 23 cm Swiss roll tin (sometimes called a jelly roll tin), greased and lined.
Essential for the ribbon stage — whisking by hand takes 15 minutes and is exhausting.

glossaryTerms.definition: The point in whisking eggs and sugar together when the mixture falls from the whisk in a thick, pale ribbon that holds its shape on the surface for 3–4 seconds before sinking. It takes about 8 minutes at high speed with a hand mixer. Reaching ribbon stage before the flour is added is what gives a Swiss roll its light, open crumb.

content.text: The Swiss roll is an exercise in timing. The sponge bakes in 12 minutes. It must come out of the tin immediately, be inverted onto a sheet of sugar-dusted baking paper, and be rolled up while it is still warm enough to flex without cracking — within the first 5 minutes of coming out of the oven. After that the sponge cools in its rolled shape, which sets the curl so it won't spring back when you unroll and re-fill it.
The batter is a whisked sponge: eggs and sugar beaten to the

, then flour folded in with a light hand. No butter; no oil. The lightness comes entirely from the air beaten into the eggs. Under-whisked eggs or over-mixed flour both produce a sponge that cracks when rolled. A hand mixer and a digital timer are the two indispensable tools.

items.prepNote: plus extra for dusting the paper
strawberry or raspberry jam — loose, not set-firm

content.text: Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Grease a 33 × 23 cm Swiss roll tin and line with baking paper, pressing it into the corners. Have a second sheet of baking paper ready on the work surface, dusted generously with caster sugar — this is what the sponge inverts onto immediately after baking.
Whisk eggs and sugar to ribbon stage

Whisk the {{eggs}} eggs and {{caster-sugar}} caster sugar together in a large bowl on high speed for 8 minutes, until the mixture is very pale, has roughly tripled in volume, and reaches the ribbon stage: the mixture falls from the whisk in a thick pale ribbon that holds on the surface for 3–4 seconds. Add the {{vanilla-extract}}.

Sift the {{plain-flour}} over the egg mixture in two additions. Fold with a large metal spoon in a figure-of-eight motion, cutting to the bottom of the bowl and turning over — about 10 folds per addition. Stop as soon as no dry flour remains. Over-folding deflates the air; an under-folded batter will have dry pockets that tear when rolled.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin and tilt gently to spread it into the corners. Bake for 10–12 minutes, until the sponge is golden, feels set when pressed in the centre, and has pulled away slightly from the tin edges. It will not brown deeply.

Invert the sponge immediately onto the sugar-dusted baking paper. Peel off the lining paper from the base of the sponge. Using the sugar paper as a guide, roll the sponge up tightly from one short end, rolling the paper inside the sponge to prevent it sticking to itself. The roll must happen within 5 minutes of coming out of the oven; once cold the sponge cracks. Leave the rolled sponge seam-side down to cool completely — about 30 minutes.

Unroll the cooled sponge carefully. Spread the {{jam}} jam over the surface, leaving a 1 cm border at the far short end. Whip the {{double-cream}} double cream to soft peaks and spread it evenly over the jam. Re-roll the sponge from the same end, using the paper to guide the roll and to keep the shape tight. The seam goes on the base. Dust with icing sugar before serving.
